ICtoolkit 0.1.0
  • Get started
  • Reference
  • Changelog

Getting started with ICtoolkit

Source: vignettes/ICtoolkit.Rmd
ICtoolkit.Rmd
library(ICtoolkit)
data(covid_eol)
covid_eol$race <- relevel(covid_eol$race, "White")

Overview

ICtoolkit provides a unified interface for five information criteria across four model classes:

Criterion Function Description
AIC compute_aic() Akaike (1974)
AICc compute_aicc() Small-sample correction
BIC compute_bic() Schwarz (1978)
EBIC compute_ebic() Extended BIC; Chen & Chen (2008)
RBIC compute_rbic() Grouped EBIC for structured predictors

All functions support lm, glm, glmnet, and ncvreg objects. For glmnet and ncvreg, the criterion is evaluated at every point on the regularization path (one value per lambda), which can itself be used as a selection algorithm without any stepwise fitting.

Throughout this vignette we use the covid_eol dataset bundled with the package — a synthetic dataset modeled after a study of family members of ICU patients surveyed for PTSD during the COVID-19 pandemic (n = 298).

1. Standard criteria for lm and glm 

fit <- lm(ies_total ~ age + race + ethnicity + sex, data = covid_eol)

compute_aic(fit)
#> [1] 1895.45
#> attr(,"fit_class")
#> [1] "lm"
#> attr(,"k")
#> [1] 7
#> attr(,"criterion")
#> [1] "AIC"
compute_aicc(fit)
#> [1] 1895.836
#> attr(,"fit_class")
#> [1] "lm"
#> attr(,"k")
#> [1] 7
#> attr(,"criterion")
#> [1] "AICc"
compute_bic(fit)
#> [1] 1925.027
#> attr(,"fit_class")
#> [1] "lm"
#> attr(,"k")
#> [1] 7
#> attr(,"criterion")
#> [1] "BIC"

Each result is a scalar with informative attributes:

result <- compute_bic(fit)
attr(result, "fit_class")   # model class
#> [1] "lm"
attr(result, "k")           # number of estimated parameters
#> [1] 7
attr(result, "criterion")   # which IC
#> [1] "BIC"

The same functions work for glm objects:

fit_glm <- glm(death ~ age + race + ethnicity + sex,
               data = covid_eol, family = binomial)
compute_aic(fit_glm)
#> [1] 407.5893
#> attr(,"fit_class")
#> [1] "glm"
#> attr(,"k")
#> [1] 7
#> attr(,"criterion")
#> [1] "AIC"
compute_bic(fit_glm)
#> [1] 433.4689
#> attr(,"fit_class")
#> [1] "glm"
#> attr(,"k")
#> [1] 7
#> attr(,"criterion")
#> [1] "BIC"

2. EBIC: Extended BIC

EBIC adds a sparsity-encouraging penalty to BIC:

EBIC=BIC+2γlog(Pk)\text{EBIC} = \text{BIC} + 2\,\gamma\,\log\binom{P}{k}

where PP is the total number of candidate predictors and kk is the number selected. The default γ\gamma follows Chen & Chen (2008):

γ=log(P/n)logP⋅𝟏(P≥n)\gamma = \frac{\log(P/\sqrt{n})}{\log P} \cdot \mathbf{1}(P \geq \sqrt{n})

# P = 10 candidate predictors (total, excluding intercept)
result_ebic <- compute_ebic(fit, P = 10)
result_ebic
#> [1] 1925.027
#> attr(,"fit_class")
#> [1] "lm"
#> attr(,"k")
#> [1] 7
#> attr(,"criterion")
#> [1] "EBIC"
#> attr(,"gamma")
#> [1] 0
#> attr(,"P")
#> [1] 10

attr(result_ebic, "gamma")   # the computed gamma
#> [1] 0
attr(result_ebic, "P")
#> [1] 10

You can supply your own gamma:

# Fixed gamma = 0.5
compute_ebic(fit, P = 10, gamma = 0.5)
#> [1] 1930.374
#> attr(,"fit_class")
#> [1] "lm"
#> attr(,"k")
#> [1] 7
#> attr(,"criterion")
#> [1] "EBIC"
#> attr(,"gamma")
#> [1] 0.5
#> attr(,"P")
#> [1] 10

Setting gamma = 0 recovers standard BIC:

all.equal(
  as.numeric(compute_ebic(fit, P = 10, gamma = 0)),
  as.numeric(compute_bic(fit))
)
#> [1] TRUE

3. RBIC: Grouped EBIC for structured predictors

RBIC generalises EBIC to predictors with a natural group structure (e.g., main effects vs. interactions, or predictors from different data modalities):

RBIC=BIC+2∑g=1Gγglog(Pgkg)\text{RBIC} = \text{BIC} + 2 \sum_{g=1}^{G} \gamma_g \log\binom{P_g}{k_g}

Groups are defined via a P_index list. For lm/glm objects, each element should be a character vector of term labels exactly as returned by attr(terms(fit), "term.labels").

# Two groups: demographic variables and race/ethnicity variables
P_index <- list(
  demographic = c("age", "sex"),
  race_eth    = c("race", "ethnicity")
)

result_rbic <- compute_rbic(fit, P_index = P_index)
result_rbic
#> [1] 1925.027
#> attr(,"fit_class")
#> [1] "lm"
#> attr(,"k")
#> [1] 7
#> attr(,"criterion")
#> [1] "RBIC"
#> attr(,"gamma")
#> [1] 0
#> attr(,"P_index")
#> attr(,"P_index")$demographic
#> [1] "age" "sex"
#> 
#> attr(,"P_index")$race_eth
#> [1] "race"      "ethnicity"

attr(result_rbic, "gamma")
#> [1] 0

4. IC over the regularization path with glmnet

For glmnet objects, all five criteria return a vector of length length(lambda). This makes IC-based selection straightforward: pick the lambda that minimises the criterion.

library(glmnet)
#> Loading required package: Matrix
#> Loaded glmnet 4.1-10

# Create a model matrix from the covid_eol predictors
X <- model.matrix(~ age + race + ethnicity + sex, data = covid_eol)[, -1]
y <- covid_eol$ies_total
p <- ncol(X)

fit_glmnet <- glmnet(X, y, family = "gaussian")

bic_path  <- compute_bic(fit_glmnet)
ebic_path <- compute_ebic(fit_glmnet, P = p)
rbic_path <- compute_rbic(fit_glmnet,
                           P_index = list(
                             demographic = c("age", "sexMale"),
                             race_eth    = c("raceBlack", "raceAAPI/NA/NI",
                                             "raceOther", "ethnicityNon-Hispanic")))

# Lambda selected by each criterion
lambda_bic  <- attr(bic_path,  "lambda")[which.min(bic_path)]
lambda_ebic <- attr(ebic_path, "lambda")[which.min(ebic_path)]
lambda_rbic <- attr(rbic_path, "lambda")[which.min(rbic_path)]

cat("BIC-selected lambda: ",  round(lambda_bic,  4), "\n")
#> BIC-selected lambda:  0.4564
cat("EBIC-selected lambda:", round(lambda_ebic, 4), "\n")
#> EBIC-selected lambda: 0.4564
cat("RBIC-selected lambda:", round(lambda_rbic, 4), "\n")
#> RBIC-selected lambda: 0.4564

# Coefficients at BIC-optimal lambda
coef(fit_glmnet, s = lambda_bic)
#> 7 x 1 sparse Matrix of class "dgCMatrix"
#>                       s=0.4563982
#> (Intercept)             14.259953
#> age                      .       
#> raceAAPI/NA/NI           .       
#> raceBlack                .       
#> raceOther                .       
#> ethnicityNon-Hispanic   -2.304095
#> sexMale                 -2.147040

Note on alpha < 1: When a glmnet model is fit with alpha < 1 (elastic-net or ridge), the package issues a warning because the count of nonzero coefficients may not be an appropriate measure of model complexity under L2 shrinkage.

5. Expanding scope with expand_scope()

When searching for interactions and polynomials, the number of candidate terms grows quickly and constructing the P_index by hand becomes tedious. expand_scope() automates this: given a base formula, it generates the expanded formula and a P_index grouped by term type.

# Expand to pairwise interactions + quadratic terms
es <- expand_scope(ies_total ~ age + race + ethnicity + sex,
                   data = covid_eol, k = 2, poly = 2)
es$formula
#> ies_total ~ age + race + ethnicity + sex + I(age^2) + age:race + 
#>     age:ethnicity + age:sex + race:ethnicity + race:sex + ethnicity:sex
#> <environment: 0x55e302a65400>
es$P_index
#> $main
#> [1] "age"       "race"      "ethnicity" "sex"      
#> 
#> $poly_2
#> [1] "I(age^2)"
#> 
#> $interact_2
#> [1] "age:race"       "age:ethnicity"  "age:sex"        "race:ethnicity"
#> [5] "race:sex"       "ethnicity:sex"

6. Stepwise selection with ic_step()

ic_step() extends MASS::stepAIC to all five criteria for lm/glm objects. Combined with expand_scope(), it makes RBIC-guided stepwise selection through interactions straightforward.

fit_null <- lm(ies_total ~ 1, data = covid_eol)

# Forward BIC selection through pairwise interactions + quadratic terms
selected <- ic_step(
  fit_null,
  scope     = list(lower = ~ 1, upper = es$formula),
  direction = "forward",
  criterion = "BIC",
  trace     = 1
)
#> Start:  BIC = 1935.9572
#> Step  1: + ethnicity:sex                     BIC = 1916.3122
#> No improvement at step 2. Stopping.
formula(selected)
#> ies_total ~ ethnicity:sex

Inspecting the selection path

ic_step() attaches a step_path data frame to the returned model:

attr(selected, "step_path")
#>   step          action      BIC
#> 1    0         <start> 1935.957
#> 2    1 + ethnicity:sex 1916.312

7. Comparing criteria on a simulation

The ranked sparsity advantage of RBIC is most apparent when the candidate set includes many derived variables (interactions, polynomials) that inflate the search space. We illustrate with a simulation where the true model has 10 main-effect candidates but only 3 active main effects and 1 active interaction — a sparse signal embedded in 65 candidate terms.

set.seed(42)
n <- 200; p <- 10
X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p,
            dimnames = list(NULL, paste0("x", 1:p)))
y <- 2*X[,1] - 1.5*X[,2] + X[,3] + 1.5*X[,1]*X[,2] + rnorm(n, sd = 2)
sim_data <- as.data.frame(cbind(y = y, X))

sim_es <- expand_scope(
  y ~ x1 + x2 + x3 + x4 + x5 + x6 + x7 + x8 + x9 + x10,
  data = sim_data, k = 2, poly = 2
)
# 10 main + 10 quadratic + 45 pairwise = 65 candidates
sapply(sim_es$P_index, length)
#>       main     poly_2 interact_2 
#>         10         10         45

We split into training and test sets, then compare backward stepwise selection under AIC, BIC, and RBIC:

set.seed(2026)
train_idx <- sample(n, n %/% 2)
train <- sim_data[train_idx, ]
test  <- sim_data[-train_idx, ]

fit_full <- lm(sim_es$formula, data = train)
scope    <- list(lower = ~ 1, upper = sim_es$formula)

true_terms <- c("x1", "x2", "x3", "x1:x2")

criteria <- c("AIC", "BIC", "RBIC")
results  <- lapply(criteria, function(crit) {
  extra_args <- list()
  if (crit == "RBIC") extra_args <- list(P_index = sim_es$P_index)
  do.call(ic_step, c(
    list(object    = fit_full,
         scope     = scope,
         direction = "backward",
         criterion = crit,
         trace     = 0),
    extra_args
  ))
})
names(results) <- criteria

# Count terms by type, false positives, and test-set RMSPE
summary_tab <- t(sapply(results, function(m) {
  sel  <- attr(terms(m), "term.labels")
  pred <- predict(m, newdata = test)
  rmspe <- sqrt(mean((test$y - pred)^2))
  c(main       = sum(sel %in% sim_es$P_index$main),
    poly       = sum(sel %in% sim_es$P_index$poly_2),
    interact   = sum(sel %in% sim_es$P_index$interact_2),
    total      = length(sel),
    true_pos   = sum(sel %in% true_terms),
    false_pos  = sum(!sel %in% true_terms),
    RMSPE      = round(rmspe, 3))
}))
summary_tab
#>      main poly interact total true_pos false_pos RMSPE
#> AIC     5    3       15    28        4        24 4.486
#> BIC     4    3       14    21        4        17 4.037
#> RBIC    3    0        1     4        4         0 2.074

RBIC recovers the true model exactly — 4 true positives with 0 false positives — and achieves by far the best prediction accuracy. BIC retains many spurious interactions and polynomials despite its heavier penalty, because it treats all 65 candidates with equal skepticism. RBIC’s group-specific penalties allow the true interaction (x1:x2) to enter while correctly excluding the 44 noise interactions.

References

Akaike, H. (1974). A new look at the statistical model identification. IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control, 19(6), 716–723.

Chen, J., & Chen, Z. (2008). Extended Bayesian information criteria for model selection with large model spaces. Biometrika, 95(3), 759–771.

Hurvich, C. M., & Tsai, C.-L. (1989). Regression and time series model selection in small samples. Biometrika, 76(2), 297–307.

Schwarz, G. (1978). Estimating the dimension of a model. The Annals of Statistics, 6(2), 461–464.

Developed by Ryan A Peterson.

Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
